DATA620-W6

Author

Ana Collado

Here is a dataset that shows a simple 2-node network: the attendance of 18 Southern Women at 14 social events.

Small “musty” datasets like that from this 1941 study have proven very valuable in testing and comparing new network algorithms.

Click here for Python code to create dataset

What can you infer about the relationships between (1) the women, and (2) the social events?

Two Mode Networks- Davis Social Southern Club Women

import networkx as nx
from networkx.algorithms import bipartite
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import pandas as pd
import numpy as np
import seaborn as sns
from plotly import graph_objects as go
from networkx.drawing.nx_agraph import graphviz_layout

Data Load

graph1= nx.davis_southern_women_graph()
#--adding some distinction--
nodecolor=["#E0E446" if graph1.nodes[n]["bipartite"]== 0 else "#F5722B" for n in graph1.nodes()]

#-- visualizing the graph--
plt.figure(figsize= (12,12))
nx.draw(graph1, with_labels=True, node_color= nodecolor, node_size=3000, edge_color= "#334ED8", font_size=12)
plt.show()

Checking out the list and attributes:

nodes1= graph1.nodes(data=True)
edges1= graph1.edges(data=True)

I do prefer to see data as a dataframe, so I’ll create two data

nodes1= pd.DataFrame.from_dict(dict(graph1.nodes(data=True), orient= "index"))
nodes1.head()

edges1= nx.to_pandas_edgelist(graph1)
edges1.head()
source target
0 Evelyn Jefferson E1
1 Evelyn Jefferson E2
2 Evelyn Jefferson E3
3 Evelyn Jefferson E4
4 Evelyn Jefferson E5
